text: The surgeon begins the procedure with sharp dissection to lyse adhesions, revealing the right sciatic hernia defect measuring approximately one by one centimeters. The right ovary and fallopian tube are adjacent to the defect, while the right ureter is located inferior and medial to it. The right iliac vessels are visible lateral and superior to the hernia defect.
The surgeon inspects the left side, confirming the absence of a sciatic hernia. Additionally, there is no visual evidence of inguinal hernias on either side.

The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap to access the preperitoneal space for hernia repair. An incision is made in the peritoneum, followed by dissection of the preperitoneal space to form a pocket for mesh placement. The right sciatic nerve is identified during dissection, with careful attention to avoid injury to the ureter and iliac vessels.
The surgeon reduces the hernia sac, revealing its depth to be approximately two to three centimeters, which is significantly larger than its one-centimeter diameter.

The surgeon places a single stitch to approximate the preperitoneal fat, protecting the nerve and ureter from the mesh. A 3x3 cm monofilament soft mesh is positioned inside the peritoneal pocket, ensuring it lies flat and covers the entire hernia defect, completely covered by the peritoneal flap.
The surgeon uses a barbed suture to close the peritoneal flap, securing the mesh in place and incorporating the hernia sac into the peritoneal closure.
